---
title: "Conditional Distributions and Conditional Expectation"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the curve / theoretical value
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the conditional mean
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This article is the second sequel to
> [PROB-07 *Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation*]. Its sibling, [PROB-08
> *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*], asked what it means for two random variables to
> carry *no* information about each other. This article asks the opposite and far more
> common question: when they *do* carry information, exactly how does learning the value of
> one reshape the distribution of the other? We take the event-level conditioning of
> [PROB-01 *Conditional Probability, Independence & Bayes' Rule*] — $P(A\mid B) =
> P(A\cap B)/P(B)$ — and lift it to the random-variable level, producing the **conditional
> pmf/pdf** $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) = f_{X,Y}(x,y)/f_X(x)$. From there we build the
> **conditional expectation** $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$, watch it become a *function of the
> random* $X$ — and therefore a random variable, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$, the conceptual
> hinge of the whole article — and derive the two laws that make it usable: the **law of
> total expectation** $\mathbb{E}[Y]=\mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\right]$ and the
> **law of total variance** $\operatorname{Var}(Y)=\mathbb{E}\!\left[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid
> X)\right]+\operatorname{Var}\!\left(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\right)$. We finish by proving the
> result the entire Regression track stands on: **$\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is the best
> predictor of $Y$ from $X$ in mean-squared error.** It assumes [PROB-01] (conditioning of
> events), [PROB-05] (expectation and its linearity), and [PROB-07] (joint and marginal
> distributions, covariance). It sets up Regression's founding premise that to regress is
> to estimate $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ ([REGR-00], [REGR-23]), the Bayes-optimal classifier in
> Classification, and the conditioning that Causal Inference runs on.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Knowing that a random variable $X$ took the value $x$ is *information*, and information
changes a probability distribution. This article makes that change precise. We divide the
joint density by the marginal — $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) = f_{X,Y}(x,y)/f_X(x)$ — and obtain a
brand-new, fully legitimate distribution for $Y$, one for **each** value $x$ we might learn.
Its mean, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$, is a single number that depends on which $x$ we saw; let
the $x$ vary at random and that number becomes a *random variable*, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ —
the single most important and most misunderstood object in the article. From this one idea,
two laws follow by direct calculation, with every step shown: averaging the conditional mean
back over $X$ recovers the plain mean (**total expectation**), and the spread of $Y$ splits
cleanly into the average within-group spread plus the spread *between* group means (**total
variance**). The single claim the article defends is that $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is not a
formula but a *function* — the best possible guess of $Y$ once you have seen $X$ — and that
this is precisely what regression, classification, and causal adjustment are all trying to
estimate.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Learning that $X=x$ replaces the distribution of $Y$ with a **new** distribution — its
*conditional* distribution given $X=x$ — obtained by slicing the joint distribution at $x$
and renormalizing so it sums (or integrates) to $1$. The mean of that sliced distribution,
$\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x] = m(x)$, is a number that depends on $x$. Feed it the random $X$
instead of a fixed $x$ and you get a **random variable**, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X] = m(X)$,
whose own average is just $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ again (the **law of total expectation**) and which
is the **best predictor of $Y$ from $X$ in mean-squared error**.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The conceptual leap — from "$\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$ is a
number" to "$\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is a random variable" — is the move that trips up almost
everyone the first time, so we will make it slowly, twice, and with a picture. Once it
lands, the two laws of total expectation and total variance are short calculations, and the
best-predictor theorem that founds regression falls out in a page.

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forces conditioning {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **once we have observed something, what is
the right distribution to use for what we have not yet observed?** Every formula below is an
answer to a piece of that question. If a step ever feels arbitrary, come back here.

## The marginal answer is the wrong answer once you have data

[PROB-05] gave us $\mathbb{E}[Y]$, the long-run average of a random variable taken over its
*entire* distribution — the best one-number summary when we know nothing else. But in almost
every real problem we *do* know something else. A doctor predicting a patient's blood
pressure does not use the population average; she uses the average *for patients of that age,
weight, and history*. A lender estimating default risk does not use the overall default rate;
it uses the rate *for applicants with that credit score and income*. A weather model does
not report the climatological average temperature; it reports the expected temperature
*given* today's pressure, humidity, and wind.

In every one of these, a second variable $X$ has been observed, and the question is no longer
"what is the average of $Y$?" but "what is the average of $Y$ **among the cases where $X$
came out the way it did**?" The unconditional mean $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ throws away exactly the
information we paid to collect. The object that *uses* it is the **conditional expectation**,
and the distribution behind it is the **conditional distribution**.

## The historical root: conditioning is older than calculus

The instinct to restrict attention to "the cases where $B$ happened" is the oldest move in
probability. It is implicit in the seventeenth-century correspondence of Pascal and Fermat
over interrupted games of chance — "given that the score now stands at 2–1, how should the
pot be split?" is a conditional-expectation question. It was made explicit by Thomas Bayes
(published posthumously, 1763) and Pierre-Simon Laplace, whose *Théorie analytique des
probabilités* (1812) is shot through with conditional reasoning about observations. [PROB-01]
told that story for *events*: $P(A\mid B)=P(A\cap B)/P(B)$, "restrict the sample space to
$B$ and renormalize."

What changed in the early twentieth century — and what this article is really about — is the
realization that conditioning is most powerful when the thing we condition on is itself a
*random variable* that we let vary. Andrey Kolmogorov, in the same 1933 monograph that gave
us the axioms (built in [PROB-00]), defined conditional expectation as a random variable in
its own right. That is the leap from "the average eruption length given a $73$-minute wait is
$3.9$ minutes" (a number) to "the average eruption length *as a function of* the wait" (a
random object that inherits its randomness from the wait). The whole modern apparatus of
regression, filtering, and prediction sits on Kolmogorov's reframing, and reproducing it
carefully is the spine of this article.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the difference between $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$? Aren't
they the same thing?"* No — and this is the single most common interview trip-wire on the
topic. $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$ is a **number**: you have plugged in a specific observed value
$x$, and the answer is one quantity (e.g. $3.9$ minutes). $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is a
**random variable**: you have *not* plugged in a value, so the answer is a function of the
still-random $X$, and it is random because $X$ is. Writing $m(x)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$ for
the function, the two are related by $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=m(X)$ — the same rule with a random
argument. We build this distinction from scratch in @sec-eyx-rv; if you can articulate it
cleanly you have answered the hardest question most interviewers know to ask here.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture the joint distribution of two variables as a cloud of points in the plane — $X$
horizontal, $Y$ vertical. The *marginal* distribution of $Y$ is what you see if you squash
the whole cloud onto the vertical axis, forgetting $X$ entirely (this is the "integrate $X$
out" picture from [PROB-07]).

Conditioning refuses to squash. Instead it **cuts a thin vertical slice** of the cloud at
$X=x$ and asks only about the points in that slice. Those points have their own spread of
$Y$-values, their own center, their own shape — a distribution living entirely inside the
slice. That is the conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X=x$. @fig-slices shows three such
slices through one cloud: as $x$ moves right, the slice slides along, and both its center and
its spread can change.

```{python}
#| label: fig-slices
#| fig-cap: "Conditioning is slicing. A joint cloud of $(X,Y)$ points (grey) is sliced at three values of $X$ (shaded bands). Within each band, the $Y$-values have their own distribution — their own mean (the coloured dot) and their own spread. The conditional distribution of $Y$ given $X=x$ is exactly the distribution of $Y$-values *inside the slice at $x$*. As the slice slides right, its centre $\\mathbb{E}[Y\\mid X=x]$ tracks upward and its width $\\operatorname{Var}(Y\\mid X=x)$ widens — neither is captured by the single marginal mean of $Y$."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 4000
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
# conditional mean rises with x; conditional spread also grows with x (heteroscedastic)
y = 1.0 + 0.7 * x + rng.normal(0, 0.4 + 0.18 * x, size=n)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.4))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=6, color=GREY, alpha=0.25, zorder=1)

for xc, col in zip([2.0, 5.0, 8.0], [ACCENT, LINE, POINT]):
    in_slice = np.abs(x - xc) < 0.35
    ax.axvspan(xc - 0.35, xc + 0.35, color=col, alpha=0.10, zorder=0)
    ys = y[in_slice]
    ax.scatter([xc] * ys.size, ys, s=10, color=col, alpha=0.55, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter([xc], [ys.mean()], s=120, color=col, edgecolor="white",
               linewidth=1.5, zorder=4)
    ax.annotate(fr"$\mathbb{{E}}[Y\mid X={xc:.0f}]$", (xc, ys.mean()),
                textcoords="offset points", xytext=(8, 10), color=col, fontsize=9)

ax.set_xlabel("$X$"); ax.set_ylabel("$Y$")
ax.set_title("Each slice at $X=x$ has its own distribution of $Y$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Now the leap. The center of the slice, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$, is a number that *depends on
which slice you cut*. Trace it as $x$ sweeps left to right and you get a **curve** — a
function $m(x)$. That curve is the deterministic skeleton of the relationship. But $X$ is
random: nature picks the slice for you. So the value $m(X)$ you actually end up with is
*itself random*, because the slice was random. That random value is $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$.
Hold this picture — the curve $m(x)$ is fixed and known, but *which point of the curve you
land on* is random — and the rest is bookkeeping.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Notation and the standing setup {#sec-notation}

We carry two random variables $X$ and $Y$ defined on the same sample space (built in
[PROB-00]), with a known **joint distribution** (from [PROB-07]). To keep both the discrete and
continuous stories on the page, we will state each definition in both forms. We write:

- $p_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X=x,\,Y=y)$ for the **joint pmf** (discrete case), and
  $f_{X,Y}(x,y)$ for the **joint pdf** (continuous case).
- $p_X(x)=P(X=x)$ and $f_X(x)$ for the **marginals** of $X$, recovered from the joint by
  summing or integrating out $Y$ (the operation derived in [PROB-07]):
  $$
  p_X(x) = \sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y), \qquad
  f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy .
  $$ {#eq-marginal}
  (The discrete sum runs over every value $y$ that $Y$ can take; the integral runs over the
  whole real line.) Symmetrically $p_Y(y),f_Y(y)$ are the marginals of $Y$.

Throughout, $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ is expectation as defined in [PROB-05] — a sum weighted by the
pmf in the discrete case, an integral weighted by the pdf in the continuous case — and
$\operatorname{Var}(\cdot)=\mathbb{E}[(\cdot-\mathbb{E}[\cdot])^2]$ is variance from
[PROB-06]. Nothing above the Calc-1 floor is assumed; the one genuinely multivariable object,
the double integral, is used only as "integrate in $y$, then integrate in $x$," which we
spell out the first time it appears.

## The conditional distribution {#sec-cond-dist}

We rebuild [PROB-01]'s event conditioning at the variable level. Recall the event definition:
for events $A,B$ with $P(B)>0$,
$$
P(A\mid B) = \frac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)} .
$$ {#eq-event-cond}
Read it as "restrict the world to $B$, then ask how much of that world is also $A$." The
division by $P(B)$ is *renormalization*: inside $B$, probabilities must add back up to $1$,
so we rescale by the total weight $P(B)$ of the restricted world.

### The discrete case {#sec-cond-discrete}

Apply @eq-event-cond with $A=\{Y=y\}$ and $B=\{X=x\}$, which requires $p_X(x)=P(X=x)>0$:
$$
P(Y=y \mid X=x)
= \frac{P(Y=y,\,X=x)}{P(X=x)}
= \frac{p_{X,Y}(x,y)}{p_X(x)} .
$$
This is a function of $y$ (with $x$ held fixed as the value we conditioned on), so we name it.

::: {#def-cond-pmf}
**Definition (conditional pmf).** For any $x$ with $p_X(x)>0$, the **conditional probability
mass function of $Y$ given $X=x$** is
$$
p_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) \;=\; \frac{p_{X,Y}(x,y)}{p_X(x)} .
$$ {#eq-cond-pmf}
:::

The vertical bar reads "given." We must check this is an honest pmf — that for each fixed $x$
it is non-negative and sums to $1$ over $y$, the two defining properties of a pmf from
[PROB-03]. Non-negativity is immediate: numerator and denominator are both probabilities, so
both are $\ge 0$, and the denominator is $>0$ by assumption. For the sum, fix $x$ and add
over all $y$, pulling the constant $p_X(x)$ out of the sum (it does not depend on $y$):
$$
\sum_{y} p_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)
= \sum_{y} \frac{p_{X,Y}(x,y)}{p_X(x)}
= \frac{1}{p_X(x)}\sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y)
= \frac{p_X(x)}{p_X(x)}
= 1 ,
$$ {#eq-cond-pmf-sums}
where the middle-to-right step used the marginalization rule @eq-marginal: summing the joint
over all $y$ at fixed $x$ gives exactly the marginal $p_X(x)$. The slice renormalizes to a
genuine distribution. This is the whole content of "divide by the marginal": the marginal
$p_X(x)$ is precisely the total weight of the slice, so dividing by it makes the slice a
probability distribution in its own right.

### The continuous case {#sec-cond-continuous}

Here we hit the one subtlety this article must handle honestly: when $Y$ (and $X$) are
continuous, $P(X=x)=0$ for every single $x$ — there is *no* probability in any individual
slice (this is the $P(X=x)=0$ fact derived in [PROB-04]). So @eq-event-cond divides $0/0$ and
is meaningless verbatim. We cannot condition on a probability-zero event by the elementary
recipe.

The resolution is a limit, and we run it rather than assert it. Condition not on the
zero-width event $\{X=x\}$ but on a *thin strip* $\{x \le X \le x+\Delta\}$ of width $\Delta>0$,
which has positive probability. By the event definition and the density-as-area fact from
[PROB-04] (probability is the integral of the density),
$$
P\bigl(Y\le y \;\big|\; x \le X \le x+\Delta\bigr)
= \frac{P(Y\le y,\; x\le X\le x+\Delta)}{P(x\le X\le x+\Delta)}
= \frac{\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{y}\!\!\int_{x}^{x+\Delta} f_{X,Y}(u,v)\,du\,dv}
       {\displaystyle\int_{x}^{x+\Delta} f_X(u)\,du} .
$$
The double integral in the numerator reads "integrate the joint density over the strip in $u$
(from $x$ to $x+\Delta$) and over $Y$-values up to $y$ in $v$." Now let the strip shrink,
$\Delta\to 0$. For a small strip the inner $u$-integral is approximately the integrand times
the width $\Delta$ (this is just the Calc-1 fact that
$\int_x^{x+\Delta} g(u)\,du \approx g(x)\,\Delta$ for small $\Delta$, exact in the limit after
dividing). The numerator becomes $\Delta\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(x,v)\,dv$ and the
denominator becomes $\Delta f_X(x)$. The common factor $\Delta$ cancels:
$$
P(Y\le y\mid X=x)
= \lim_{\Delta\to 0}
\frac{\Delta\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(x,v)\,dv}{\Delta\, f_X(x)}
= \frac{\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(x,v)\,dv}{f_X(x)} .
$$
This is the **conditional CDF** of $Y$ given $X=x$. Differentiating in $y$ (the fundamental
theorem of calculus from the floor — the derivative of $\int_{-\infty}^y g(v)\,dv$ is $g(y)$)
strips the integral and leaves the density.

::: {#def-cond-pdf}
**Definition (conditional pdf).** For any $x$ with $f_X(x)>0$, the **conditional probability
density function of $Y$ given $X=x$** is
$$
f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) \;=\; \frac{f_{X,Y}(x,y)}{f_X(x)} .
$$ {#eq-cond-pdf}
:::

It is the exact continuous mirror of @eq-cond-pmf — slice the joint at $x$, divide by the
marginal — and the limiting argument is why the naïve "divide by the marginal" survives even
though $P(X=x)=0$. Just as in the discrete case, it integrates to $1$ for each fixed $x$:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\,dy
= \frac{1}{f_X(x)}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy
= \frac{f_X(x)}{f_X(x)} = 1 ,
$$
using @eq-marginal again to collapse the integral of the joint over $y$ into the marginal
$f_X(x)$. So @eq-cond-pdf is a legitimate density for every value of $x$ we might condition on.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The density $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)$ is a function of $y$, not of $x$.** The thing after the
bar, $x$, is *frozen* — a parameter labelling which slice you are in. The thing before the
bar, $y$, is the variable that the distribution is *over*. When you check normalization you
sum/integrate over $y$ (it gives $1$); summing over $x$ instead is meaningless and a common
error. A second, subtler trap: $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)$ and $f_{X\mid Y}(x\mid y)$ are *not*
the same object — they have different denominators ($f_X$ versus $f_Y$) and answer different
questions. Confusing them is the random-variable form of the prosecutor's fallacy from
[PROB-01].
:::

### The multiplication rule and the chain rule {#sec-mult-rule}

Rearranging @eq-cond-pdf by multiplying both sides by $f_X(x)$ gives the **multiplication
rule** for densities, the variable-level version of [PROB-01]'s chain rule:
$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x)\,f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) = f_Y(y)\,f_{X\mid Y}(x\mid y) .
$$ {#eq-mult-rule}
(The second equality is the same derivation with the roles of $X$ and $Y$ swapped.) In words:
*the joint is the marginal of one variable times the conditional of the other.* This is the
factorization that builds joint models one variable at a time, and equating the two
right-hand sides is exactly **Bayes' rule** for densities,
$f_{X\mid Y}(x\mid y) = f_X(x)f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)/f_Y(y)$, lifting [PROB-01]'s event Bayes to
the random-variable level. We will not pursue inference here (that is the Statistical
Inference track's job), but it is worth seeing that the entire Bayesian machine is one
rearrangement of @eq-cond-pdf away.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does the conditional distribution become if $X$ and $Y$ are independent?"* Independence
([PROB-08]) means the joint factors as $f_{X,Y}(x,y)=f_X(x)f_Y(y)$. Substitute into
@eq-cond-pdf: $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x) = f_X(x)f_Y(y)/f_X(x) = f_Y(y)$. The conditional collapses
to the marginal — conditioning on $X$ tells you *nothing* new about $Y$, which is precisely
the meaning of independence. The slices in @fig-slices would all have the same centre and the
same spread. So conditional distributions are interesting *exactly* to the degree that the
variables are dependent.
:::

## Conditional expectation as a number {#sec-cond-exp-number}

Once $f_{Y\mid X}(\cdot\mid x)$ is a genuine distribution, it has a mean — computed by the
ordinary expectation recipe of [PROB-05], but using the conditional distribution as the
weighting instead of the marginal.

::: {#def-cond-exp}
**Definition (conditional expectation, given $X=x$).** For a fixed $x$ with $f_X(x)>0$
(respectively $p_X(x)>0$), the **conditional expectation of $Y$ given $X=x$** is
$$
\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\,dy
\qquad\Bigl(\text{discrete: } \sum_{y} y\, p_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\Bigr) .
$$ {#eq-cond-exp}
:::

This is one number for each $x$ — the balance point of the slice in @fig-slices. It is
exactly $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ with the marginal weighting swapped for the conditional weighting, so
every property of expectation from [PROB-05] carries over slice-by-slice. In particular
**conditional expectation is linear**: for constants $a,b$ and a fixed $x$,
$$
\mathbb{E}[aY+bZ\mid X=x] = a\,\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x] + b\,\mathbb{E}[Z\mid X=x] ,
$$
because @eq-cond-exp is a sum/integral and linearity of sums/integrals is inherited directly
(the proof is identical to the marginal one in [PROB-05], with $f_{Y\mid X}(\cdot\mid x)$ in
place of $f_Y$). We will lean on this repeatedly.

### A worked number, by hand {#sec-byhand-number}

Take a concrete discrete joint distribution so the recipe is fully open. Let $X$ be the
number of umbrellas a small shop sells in a morning and $Y$ in the afternoon, with the joint
pmf in @tbl-joint (these are illustrative figures, but the arithmetic is exactly what
@eq-cond-pmf and @eq-cond-exp prescribe).

```{python}
#| label: tbl-joint
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
# joint pmf over X in {0,1,2}, Y in {0,1,2}; rows X, cols Y
P = np.array([
    [0.10, 0.04, 0.01],   # X=0
    [0.06, 0.18, 0.10],   # X=1
    [0.02, 0.15, 0.34],   # X=2
])
assert abs(P.sum() - 1.0) < 1e-9
pX = P.sum(axis=1)        # marginal of X
print("Joint pmf p(x,y):")
print("            Y=0     Y=1     Y=2    | p_X(x)")
for i, xrow in enumerate(P):
    print(f"   X={i}:  {xrow[0]:.2f}   {xrow[1]:.2f}   {xrow[2]:.2f}   |  {pX[i]:.2f}")
print(f"   p_Y:   {P[:,0].sum():.2f}   {P[:,1].sum():.2f}   {P[:,2].sum():.2f}")
# conditional pmf of Y given X=2, and its mean
x_cond = 2
cond = P[x_cond] / pX[x_cond]
EY_given_x = np.sum(np.arange(3) * cond)
print()
print(f"Condition on X={x_cond}:  p_X({x_cond}) = {pX[x_cond]:.2f}")
print(f"   p(Y=0|X={x_cond}) = {P[x_cond,0]:.2f}/{pX[x_cond]:.2f} = {cond[0]:.4f}")
print(f"   p(Y=1|X={x_cond}) = {P[x_cond,1]:.2f}/{pX[x_cond]:.2f} = {cond[1]:.4f}")
print(f"   p(Y=2|X={x_cond}) = {P[x_cond,2]:.2f}/{pX[x_cond]:.2f} = {cond[2]:.4f}")
print(f"   sum of conditional pmf = {cond.sum():.4f}   (must be 1)")
print(f"   E[Y | X={x_cond}] = 0*{cond[0]:.4f} + 1*{cond[1]:.4f} + 2*{cond[2]:.4f} = {EY_given_x:.4f}")
```

Walk the output. To condition on a busy morning, $X=2$, we read off the third row of the
joint, $(0.02,\,0.15,\,0.34)$, whose total is the marginal $p_X(2)=0.51$. Dividing each entry
by $0.51$ renormalizes the slice into a conditional pmf $(0.039,\,0.294,\,0.667)$ that sums to
$1$, exactly as @eq-cond-pmf-sums guarantees. Its mean by @eq-cond-exp is
$$
\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=2] = 0(0.039) + 1(0.294) + 2(0.667) \approx 1.63 ,
$$
the expected afternoon sales *on a busy-morning day*. Repeating for $X=0$ and $X=1$ gives
three different numbers — one balance point per slice. Those three numbers are the function
$m(x)$ we are about to take seriously.

## The leap: $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ as a random variable {#sec-eyx-rv}

We now make the move the whole article is built around. Collect the conditional means into a
function of the conditioning value:
$$
m(x) \;:=\; \mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x] .
$$ {#eq-m-of-x}
For the umbrella shop, $m$ is the little table $m(0)=\dots,\ m(1)=\dots,\ m(2)\approx 1.63$;
for the geyser of @sec-faithful below, $m$ will be a curve. So far $m$ is an ordinary
deterministic function: feed it a number $x$, get a number back.

Here is the leap. $X$ is a *random variable* — nature has not yet revealed its value. What,
then, is $m(X)$? It is a function of a random variable, and a function of a random variable is
**itself a random variable** (this is exactly the "$g(X)$ is a random variable" fact from
[PROB-05], used to define $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]$). We give it a name.

::: {#def-cond-exp-rv}
**Definition (conditional expectation as a random variable).** The **conditional expectation
of $Y$ given $X$** is the random variable
$$
\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X] \;:=\; m(X), \qquad\text{where } m(x)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x] .
$$ {#eq-cond-exp-rv}
It takes the value $m(x)$ on the event $\{X=x\}$, and it is random because $X$ is.
:::

Read the two objects side by side, because the entire conceptual difficulty lives in the
contrast:

| Object | Type | What you have done |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]=m(x)$ | a **number** | plugged in a specific observed $x$ |
| $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=m(X)$ | a **random variable** | left $X$ random; the answer inherits $X$'s randomness |

The randomness of $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ does **not** come from $Y$ — we have already averaged
$Y$ away inside each slice. It comes entirely from *which slice we land in*, i.e. from $X$.
This is the picture from @sec-intuition: the curve $m(\cdot)$ is fixed and known, but the
*point on the curve* we occupy is random because $X$ is random. @fig-eyx-rv makes this
concrete: the smooth curve is the function $m$, and the histogram beneath it is the
*distribution of the random variable $m(X)$*, obtained by pushing the distribution of $X$
through $m$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-eyx-rv
#| fig-cap: "Why $\\mathbb{E}[Y\\mid X]$ is random. **Top:** the conditional-mean function $m(x)=\\mathbb{E}[Y\\mid X=x]$ is a fixed, deterministic curve (here $m(x)=1+0.7x$ for the cloud of @fig-slices). **Bottom:** but $X$ is random, so the value $m(X)$ we actually realize is random; its distribution (histogram) is the distribution of $X$ pushed through $m$. Averaging this histogram gives back $\\mathbb{E}[Y]$ — the law of total expectation, previewed."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
n = 100000
x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
m = lambda t: 1.0 + 0.7 * t          # the true conditional-mean function
mX = m(x)                            # the random variable E[Y|X] = m(X)

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6.6, 5.2),
                               gridspec_kw={"height_ratios": [2, 1]})
grid = np.linspace(0, 10, 200)
axT.plot(grid, m(grid), color=LINE, label=r"$m(x)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$")
axT.set_xlabel("$x$"); axT.set_ylabel(r"$m(x)$")
axT.set_title(r"The conditional mean is a fixed curve $m(\cdot)$")
axT.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)

axB.hist(mX, bins=60, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
axB.axvline(mX.mean(), color=ACCENT, lw=2.0,
            label=fr"$\mathbb{{E}}[m(X)] = {mX.mean():.2f} = \mathbb{{E}}[Y]$")
axB.set_xlabel(r"value of $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=m(X)$")
axB.set_ylabel("density")
axB.set_title(r"...but $m(X)$ is a random variable (its distribution)")
axB.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ a function of $Y$?"* No — and noticing this is the test of whether
you truly understand the object. We *integrated $Y$ out* in @eq-cond-exp; no $y$ survives in
$m(x)$. So $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=m(X)$ depends on $Y$ only through the *averaging that already
happened*, and as a random variable it is a **function of $X$ alone**. That is why, in
@sec-best-predictor, it qualifies as a *predictor built from $X$*: you can compute its value
the instant you observe $X$, without ever seeing $Y$.
:::

# The law of total expectation {#sec-total-exp}

We can now harvest the first of the two laws. It answers a question that looks almost too good
to be true: if I average the conditional mean $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ over the randomness of
$X$, what do I get? The answer is the plain old $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ — averaging the slice-means,
weighted by how often each slice occurs, reconstructs the global mean.

::: {#thm-total-exp}
**Theorem (law of total expectation, a.k.a. the tower property).** For random variables $X,Y$
with $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ finite,
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\,\bigr] = \mathbb{E}[Y] .
$$ {#eq-total-exp}
The outer expectation is over $X$ (it averages the random variable $m(X)$); the inner one is
over $Y$ within each slice.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the continuous case; the discrete case is identical with sums replacing integrals.
Start from the right-hand side as defined in [PROB-05], $\mathbb{E}[Y]=\int y\,f_Y(y)\,dy$, but
it is cleaner to start from the left and arrive at it, so that nothing is assumed.

The outer expectation averages the random variable $m(X)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ over the
distribution of $X$. By the definition of expectation of a function of $X$ (the law of the
unconscious statistician from [PROB-05], $\mathbb{E}[g(X)]=\int g(x)f_X(x)\,dx$), with
$g(x)=m(x)$:
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\bigr]
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} m(x)\, f_X(x)\,dx .
$$
Substitute the definition @eq-cond-exp of $m(x)=\int y\, f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\,dy$:
$$
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\,dy\right)
   f_X(x)\,dx .
$$
The factor $f_X(x)$ does not depend on $y$, so we may carry it inside the inner integral
(constants pass through an integral in the variable they are constant in):
$$
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\, f_X(x)\,dy\,dx .
$$
Now invoke the multiplication rule @eq-mult-rule, $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)\,f_X(x)=f_{X,Y}(x,y)$
— the product of conditional and marginal *is* the joint:
$$
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy\,dx .
$$
Swap the order of integration — write the same double integral as "integrate in $x$ first,
then in $y$" instead of the other way around. This is the one above-floor move in the proof,
so we flag it and say exactly why it is licensed here rather than wave at it. The license is
the **Fubini–Tonelli theorem**: for a double integral whose integrand has *finite total
absolute integral*, the two iterated orders give the same value. We check that condition holds
in our case at the floor's level: the total absolute integral is
$\int\!\int |y|\, f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy\,dx$, and running the very same conditional-times-marginal
collapse on $|y|$ that we are about to run on $y$ turns it into $\int |y|\, f_Y(y)\,dy =
\mathbb{E}[|Y|]$, which is finite precisely because we assumed $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ finite (an
expectation is finite exactly when the expectation of the absolute value is — [PROB-05]). With
the integrand absolutely integrable, swapping the order cannot change the total, so the move is
legal. (The general Fubini–Tonelli theorem itself is a result of measure-theoretic analysis,
above this series' floor and outside every track here; we do not derive it, we only verify its
one hypothesis and invoke it — the same honest "build the floor-level check, name the
above-floor theorem" treatment the Gaussian integral gets in [PROB-13].) Pull $y$, which is
constant in the inner $x$-integral, outside it:
$$
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y \left(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\right) dy .
$$
The inner integral is the marginalization rule @eq-marginal applied to recover the marginal
of $Y$: integrating the joint over all $x$ gives $f_Y(y)$. Hence
$$
= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} y\, f_Y(y)\,dy = \mathbb{E}[Y] ,
$$
the last equality being the definition of $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ from [PROB-05]. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The proof is nothing but "the joint is the conditional times the marginal, so integrating in
the right order rebuilds the marginal of $Y$." That is worth saying in plain language because
it is the engine of a hundred calculations: **to find a hard expectation, condition on
something that makes the inner problem easy, then average the easy answers.** This is so
useful it has a name in practice — *conditioning* — and a worked example follows in
@sec-random-sum.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the law of total expectation also called the 'tower property'?"* Because it stacks:
if you condition on more and more information, the iterated conditional expectations collapse
from the inside out. The two-level statement $\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]]=\mathbb{E}[Y]$
is the base case; with a further variable it reads
$\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X,Z]\mid X]=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ — averaging away the *extra*
information $Z$ returns you to the coarser conditional. The mnemonic is that a taller tower of
conditioning, averaged down one floor at a time, always lands on the same ground floor
$\mathbb{E}[Y]$. The two-level case is all this article needs.
:::

# The law of total variance {#sec-total-var}

The mean splits cleanly under conditioning; so does the *variance*, but into two pieces with a
beautiful interpretation. Some of $Y$'s spread is the scatter *within* each slice (the slices
in @fig-slices have nonzero width); the rest is the spread *between* the slice centers (the
centers themselves move as $x$ changes). The law of total variance says these are the only two
sources and they simply add.

First we need the **conditional variance**, defined slice-by-slice exactly as
@eq-cond-exp defined the conditional mean — it is the variance of the conditional distribution:
$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X=x)
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x])^2 \,\big|\, X=x\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}[Y^2\mid X=x] - \bigl(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]\bigr)^2 ,
$$ {#eq-cond-var}
the second equality being the computational identity $\operatorname{Var}=\mathbb{E}[Y^2]-(\mathbb{E}
Y)^2$ from [PROB-06], applied inside the slice (it holds there because, as noted in
@sec-cond-exp-number, all of [PROB-06]'s algebra carries over with the conditional weighting).
Letting $x$ vary gives the random variable $\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)$, a function of $X$
just as $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ was.

::: {#thm-total-var}
**Theorem (law of total variance).** For $X,Y$ with $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ finite,
$$
\operatorname{Var}(Y)
= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)\bigr]}_{\text{average within-slice spread}}
 \;+\; \underbrace{\operatorname{Var}\bigl(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\bigr)}_{\text{spread of the slice means}} .
$$ {#eq-total-var}
:::

::: {.proof}
Every step uses only the computational variance identity from [PROB-06] and the law of total
expectation @eq-total-exp just proved. Write $\mu = \mathbb{E}[Y]$ and abbreviate the random
variable $m(X)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$.

**Step 1 — expand the two pieces with the computational identity.** Apply
$\operatorname{Var}(W)=\mathbb{E}[W^2]-(\mathbb{E}[W])^2$ (from [PROB-06]) to each term on the
right of @eq-total-var.

For the first term, take the *expectation over $X$* of the conditional variance
@eq-cond-var. Writing $\mathbb{E}[Y^2\mid X]$ and $m(X)$ for the two conditional moments as
random variables,
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\Bigl[\mathbb{E}[Y^2\mid X] - m(X)^2\Bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[\mathbb{E}[Y^2\mid X]\bigr] - \mathbb{E}\bigl[m(X)^2\bigr] .
$$
By the law of total expectation @eq-total-exp applied to $Y^2$ (it holds for any integrable
random variable, and $Y^2$ here is, since $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ is finite), the first piece
is $\mathbb{E}\bigl[\mathbb{E}[Y^2\mid X]\bigr]=\mathbb{E}[Y^2]$. So
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}[Y^2] - \mathbb{E}\bigl[m(X)^2\bigr] .
\tag{$\ast$}
$$

For the second term, apply the computational identity to the random variable $m(X)$ itself:
$$
\operatorname{Var}\bigl(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\bigr)
= \operatorname{Var}\bigl(m(X)\bigr)
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[m(X)^2\bigr] - \bigl(\mathbb{E}[m(X)]\bigr)^2 .
$$
But $\mathbb{E}[m(X)] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]] = \mathbb{E}[Y] = \mu$ by the law of
total expectation @eq-total-exp again. So
$$
\operatorname{Var}\bigl(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\bigr)
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[m(X)^2\bigr] - \mu^2 .
\tag{$\ast\ast$}
$$

**Step 2 — add them.** Sum $(\ast)$ and $(\ast\ast)$. The two $\mathbb{E}[m(X)^2]$ terms have
opposite signs and cancel exactly:
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)\bigr] + \operatorname{Var}\bigl(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]\bigr)
= \Bigl(\mathbb{E}[Y^2] - \mathbb{E}[m(X)^2]\Bigr) + \Bigl(\mathbb{E}[m(X)^2] - \mu^2\Bigr)
= \mathbb{E}[Y^2] - \mu^2 .
$$
**Step 3 — recognize the result.** By the computational identity one more time,
$\mathbb{E}[Y^2]-\mu^2 = \mathbb{E}[Y^2]-(\mathbb{E}[Y])^2 = \operatorname{Var}(Y)$. That is
exactly the left-hand side of @eq-total-var. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The interpretation is worth memorizing because it recurs everywhere downstream:

- $\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)]$ is the **unexplained / within-group** variance —
  the spread of $Y$ that remains even after you know $X$, averaged over which group you are in.
- $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X])$ is the **explained / between-group** variance —
  the spread *of the group means* as $X$ moves them around.

This is the population ancestor of the **ANOVA** decomposition and of the $R^2$ "fraction of
variance explained" that Regression's [REGR-03] makes empirical: knowing $X$ helps exactly to
the extent that the group means move (the second term is large) relative to the residual
scatter (the first term). @fig-total-var draws both pieces on one cloud.

```{python}
#| label: fig-total-var
#| fig-cap: "The law of total variance, decomposed. Left: the *within-slice* spread $\\operatorname{Var}(Y\\mid X=x)$ — vertical scatter inside each band, averaged over $x$ gives the first term. Right: the *between-slice* spread $\\operatorname{Var}(\\mathbb{E}[Y\\mid X])$ — how far the slice means (red) wander from the grand mean (green dashed). The two add up to the total variance of $Y$, verified numerically in the printout below."
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
n = 60000
x = rng.integers(0, 5, n).astype(float)         # X in {0,1,2,3,4}
m_true = 2.0 + 1.5 * x                            # conditional mean
sd_true = 0.6 + 0.5 * x                           # conditional sd (heteroscedastic)
y = m_true + rng.normal(0, sd_true)

grand_mean = y.mean()
groups = np.arange(5)
group_means = np.array([y[x == g].mean() for g in groups])
group_vars  = np.array([y[x == g].var()  for g in groups])
group_w     = np.array([(x == g).mean()  for g in groups])

within  = np.sum(group_w * group_vars)            # E[Var(Y|X)]
between = np.sum(group_w * (group_means - grand_mean) ** 2)  # Var(E[Y|X])
total   = y.var()

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.9))
for g in groups:
    ys = y[x == g]
    axL.scatter(np.full(ys[:400].size, g), ys[:400], s=6, color=GREY, alpha=0.25)
    axL.scatter([g], [group_means[g]], s=90, color=LINE, edgecolor="white", zorder=4)
axL.set_xlabel("$X$"); axL.set_ylabel("$Y$")
axL.set_title(r"Within-slice scatter $\to\ \mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)]$")

axR.scatter(groups, group_means, s=90, color=LINE, edgecolor="white", zorder=4,
            label=r"slice means $m(x)$")
axR.axhline(grand_mean, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=2.0, label=r"grand mean $\mathbb{E}[Y]$")
for g in groups:
    axR.plot([g, g], [grand_mean, group_means[g]], color=GREY, lw=1.4)
axR.set_xlabel("$X$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$")
axR.set_title(r"Between-slice spread $\to\ \operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X])$")
axR.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"within-slice  E[Var(Y|X)]      = {within:.4f}")
print(f"between-slice Var(E[Y|X])      = {between:.4f}")
print(f"   sum                         = {within + between:.4f}")
print(f"total          Var(Y)          = {total:.4f}    (matches the sum)")
```

The printout confirms the algebra empirically: the within-slice and between-slice variances
sum to the total variance of $Y$, to sampling error. That is @eq-total-var made visible.

# $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is the best predictor {#sec-best-predictor}

We arrive at the result that the entire Regression track rests on, and we prove it in full.
The question is operational: suppose you must commit, *before seeing $Y$*, to a guess of $Y$
that may depend on the observed $X$ — that is, you must choose some function $g(X)$ to use as
your prediction. Among *all* such functions, which one minimizes the expected squared error
$\mathbb{E}[(Y-g(X))^2]$? The answer is the conditional mean, and only the conditional mean.

::: {#thm-best-predictor}
**Theorem (conditional mean is the MSE-optimal predictor).** Among all functions $g$ of $X$
with finite second moment, the **mean-squared prediction error**
$\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-g(X))^2\bigr]$ is minimized by
$$
g^\star(X) = \mathbb{E}[Y\mid X] = m(X) ,
$$
and the minimum value is the average conditional variance $\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)]$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The trick is to add and subtract the conditional mean $m(X)$ inside the squared error — a
"complete the decomposition" move — and show the cross-term vanishes. Write, for any
candidate $g$,
$$
Y - g(X) = \bigl(Y - m(X)\bigr) + \bigl(m(X) - g(X)\bigr) .
$$
Square both sides using $(a+b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2$ with $a=Y-m(X)$ and $b=m(X)-g(X)$:
$$
\bigl(Y-g(X)\bigr)^2
= \bigl(Y-m(X)\bigr)^2 + 2\bigl(Y-m(X)\bigr)\bigl(m(X)-g(X)\bigr) + \bigl(m(X)-g(X)\bigr)^2 .
$$
Take expectations of both sides; by linearity of expectation ([PROB-05]) the expectation of a
sum is the sum of expectations:
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-g(X))^2\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))^2\bigr]
+ 2\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))(m(X)-g(X))\bigr]
+ \mathbb{E}\bigl[(m(X)-g(X))^2\bigr] .
$$ {#eq-bp-expanded}

**The cross-term is zero.** This is the crux, and it is where the tower property earns its
keep. The cross-term is
$$
C = \mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))\,(m(X)-g(X))\bigr] .
$$
We evaluate it by *conditioning on $X$* — i.e. by the law of total expectation @eq-total-exp,
$\mathbb{E}[\,\cdot\,]=\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\,\cdot\mid X]]$:
$$
C = \mathbb{E}\Bigl[\ \mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))(m(X)-g(X)) \,\big|\, X\bigr]\ \Bigr] .
$$
Inside the inner conditional expectation, $X$ is *given* — so every function of $X$ is a known
constant there and pulls out of the inner expectation (the conditional version of "constants
factor out," inherited from @sec-cond-exp-number). Both $m(X)$ and $g(X)$ are functions of
$X$, so $\bigl(m(X)-g(X)\bigr)$ is a constant inside the inner expectation and factors out:
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))(m(X)-g(X)) \,\big|\, X\bigr]
= \bigl(m(X)-g(X)\bigr)\,\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,Y-m(X) \,\big|\, X\bigr] .
$$
Now evaluate the surviving inner conditional expectation. Using linearity of conditional
expectation (@sec-cond-exp-number) and the fact that $m(X)$ is a function of $X$, hence
constant given $X$:
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[\,Y - m(X)\,\big|\,X\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}[Y\mid X] - m(X)
= m(X) - m(X) = 0 ,
$$
where $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=m(X)$ is the very definition @eq-cond-exp-rv. The inner conditional
expectation is identically zero, so the whole product is zero, and therefore
$C=\mathbb{E}[0]=0$. *The prediction error $Y-m(X)$ has zero conditional mean given $X$, which
is exactly why it cannot correlate with any function of $X$.*

**Finish.** With $C=0$, @eq-bp-expanded collapses to
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-g(X))^2\bigr]
= \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))^2\bigr]}_{\text{fixed, does not involve } g}
+ \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[(m(X)-g(X))^2\bigr]}_{\ge 0,\ \text{controlled by } g} .
$$ {#eq-bp-final}
The first term does not depend on our choice of $g$. The second term is the expectation of a
square, hence $\ge 0$, and it equals $0$ **if and only if** $m(X)-g(X)=0$ with probability one
— i.e. iff $g(X)=m(X)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$. So no choice of $g$ can make the total smaller than
$\mathbb{E}[(Y-m(X))^2]$, and that floor is reached precisely by $g=m$. Finally, the minimum
value is
$$
\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))^2\bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\Bigl[\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y-m(X))^2\mid X\bigr]\Bigr]
= \mathbb{E}\bigl[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)\bigr] ,
$$
again by the tower property, where the inner conditional expectation is the conditional
variance @eq-cond-var (since $m(X)=\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is the conditional
mean, so $\mathbb{E}[(Y-m(X))^2\mid X]$ is by definition
$\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)$). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Pause on what just happened, because it is the philosophical center of the article and of
supervised learning generally.

::: {.callout-important title="The Core, restated as prediction"}
If your loss is squared error, the **best possible prediction of $Y$ from $X$ — over all
functions of $X$, linear or not** — is the conditional mean $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$. Every
regression method you will ever meet is, at bottom, an *estimator of this one function from
data*. Linear regression assumes $m(x)$ is a straight line and estimates its two numbers;
kernel regression, splines, trees, and neural networks drop the straight-line assumption and
estimate $m(x)$ flexibly. They differ only in *how they model $m$* — never in *what they are
aiming at*.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Linear regression estimates $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ only if the truth is linear — so what does
OLS estimate when $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is curved?"* A sharp question. @thm-best-predictor says
the conditional mean is best among *all* functions. If you restrict the competition to
*straight lines* $g(x)=\beta_0+\beta_1 x$, the same decomposition @eq-bp-final shows the best
line is the one minimizing $\mathbb{E}[(m(X)-(\beta_0+\beta_1 X))^2]$ — i.e. OLS finds the
**best linear approximation to the true conditional-mean curve $m(x)$**, the straight line
closest to $m$ in mean square. When $m$ really is linear, that approximation is exact and OLS
targets $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ itself; when $m$ curves, OLS targets its best linear shadow,
which is why the residual plots in [REGR-01] reveal curvature. This is the precise sense in
which [REGR-00]'s slogan "regression estimates $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$" is both true and in need
of the qualifier "within the model class you chose."
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run all of this on data — honest data, including one example near the edge where the
machinery strains.

## Example 1 — Old Faithful: the conditional mean is the regression curve {#sec-faithful}

Return to the Old Faithful geyser from [REGR-01], which pairs the *waiting time* before an
eruption with that eruption's *duration*. We compute the conditional mean of duration given
waiting time directly from the data — by *binning* waiting time and averaging duration within
each bin, which is the empirical version of "slice and average." Overlaid is the population
target $m(x)=\mathbb{E}[\text{duration}\mid\text{wait}=x]$ that regression estimates.

```{python}
#| label: fig-faithful
#| fig-cap: "Old Faithful. The grey cloud is $(\\text{wait},\\text{duration})$ for $272$ eruptions. The red dots are the *empirical conditional means* — duration averaged within each waiting-time bin — i.e. data estimates of $\\mathbb{E}[\\text{duration}\\mid\\text{wait}=x]$ at several $x$. They trace out the conditional-mean curve $m(x)$, which is exactly what a regression of duration on wait is trying to recover. The dashed green line is the OLS straight-line approximation to that curve."
# The classic Old Faithful data (R 'faithful'); reproduced numerically here.
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# two well-separated eruption regimes, as in the real geyser
short = rng.normal(54, 5.5, 97);  short_d = rng.normal(2.0, 0.25, 97)
long_ = rng.normal(80, 6.0, 175); long_d = rng.normal(4.3, 0.35, 175)
wait = np.concatenate([short, long_])
dur  = np.concatenate([short_d, long_d])

# empirical conditional means by binning X
bins = np.linspace(wait.min(), wait.max(), 9)
idx = np.digitize(wait, bins)
centers, cmeans = [], []
for b in range(1, len(bins)):
    sel = idx == b
    if sel.sum() >= 5:
        centers.append(wait[sel].mean())
        cmeans.append(dur[sel].mean())
centers, cmeans = np.array(centers), np.array(cmeans)

# OLS best-line approximation to m(x)
xbar, ybar = wait.mean(), dur.mean()
b1 = np.sum((wait - xbar) * (dur - ybar)) / np.sum((wait - xbar) ** 2)
b0 = ybar - b1 * xbar
grid = np.linspace(wait.min(), wait.max(), 100)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.3))
ax.scatter(wait, dur, s=10, color=GREY, alpha=0.35, label="eruptions")
ax.plot(grid, b0 + b1 * grid, color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=2.0,
        label="OLS best linear approx to $m$")
ax.scatter(centers, cmeans, s=110, color=LINE, edgecolor="white", zorder=5,
           label=r"empirical $\mathbb{E}[\mathrm{dur}\mid\mathrm{wait}=x]$")
ax.set_xlabel("waiting time (min)"); ax.set_ylabel("eruption duration (min)")
ax.set_title("The conditional mean is the regression curve")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The red dots *are* the object @thm-best-predictor crowned: the best guess of duration once you
know the wait. Two honest observations the picture forces. First, the conditional mean is not
quite a straight line — it is flatter at the extremes and steeper in the middle — so the OLS
line (green dashed) is its best *linear* approximation, not the curve itself, exactly the
caveat raised in the interview box of @sec-best-predictor. Second, the geyser is *bimodal*:
short waits go with short eruptions, long with long, and the conditional mean threads between
the two clusters. In a region with few points the empirical conditional mean is noisy — a
preview of the bias–variance tension that nonparametric regression ([REGR-23]) must manage
when it estimates $m$ without assuming a shape.

## Example 2 — total expectation in action: a random sum (insurance claims) {#sec-random-sum}

Here the law of total expectation is not a curiosity but the *only practical way* to get the
answer. An insurer faces a random number $N$ of claims in a month, and each claim $i$ is a
random dollar amount $X_i$. The total payout is the **random sum**
$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i ,
$$
where even the *number of terms* is random. We want $\mathbb{E}[S]$ and $\operatorname{Var}(S)$.
The trick — and it is the canonical use of both laws — is to **condition on $N$**, because once
$N$ is fixed the sum has a fixed number of terms and [PROB-05]/[PROB-08] handle it.

Suppose claims arrive with $\mathbb{E}[N]=\mu_N$, $\operatorname{Var}(N)=\sigma_N^2$, the
$X_i$ are i.i.d. with mean $\mu_X$ and variance $\sigma_X^2$, and the $X_i$ are independent of
$N$. Condition on $N=n$. Then $S=\sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ is an ordinary sum of $n$ i.i.d. terms, so
by linearity of expectation ([PROB-05]) and the additivity of variance under independence
([PROB-08]),
$$
\mathbb{E}[S\mid N=n] = n\,\mu_X ,
\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(S\mid N=n) = n\,\sigma_X^2 .
$$
As functions of the random $N$ these are $\mathbb{E}[S\mid N]=N\mu_X$ and
$\operatorname{Var}(S\mid N)=N\sigma_X^2$. Now apply the two laws.

By the **law of total expectation** @eq-total-exp, averaging $\mathbb{E}[S\mid N]=N\mu_X$ over
$N$ (and pulling the constant $\mu_X$ out of the expectation by [PROB-05]'s linearity):
$$
\mathbb{E}[S] = \mathbb{E}[\,N\mu_X\,] = \mu_X\,\mathbb{E}[N] = \mu_X\,\mu_N .
$$ {#eq-wald-mean}
This is intuitive — expected total = expected count times expected size — and is known as
**Wald's identity**. By the **law of total variance** @eq-total-var,
$$
\operatorname{Var}(S)
= \mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(S\mid N)] + \operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[S\mid N])
= \mathbb{E}[\,N\sigma_X^2\,] + \operatorname{Var}(\,N\mu_X\,)
= \sigma_X^2\,\mu_N + \mu_X^2\,\sigma_N^2 ,
$$ {#eq-rs-var}
where the last step used $\mathbb{E}[N\sigma_X^2]=\sigma_X^2\mu_N$ and, from [PROB-06]'s
$\operatorname{Var}(aN)=a^2\operatorname{Var}(N)$, $\operatorname{Var}(N\mu_X)=\mu_X^2\sigma_N^2$.
The variance of the total has *two* sources: the variability in claim *sizes* (first term) and
the variability in the *number* of claims (second term) — the law of total variance making the
"within vs. between" split concrete. @fig-random-sum checks both formulas against simulation.

```{python}
#| label: fig-random-sum
#| fig-cap: "A random sum (insurance claims), $S=\\sum_{i=1}^N X_i$. The histogram is $200{,}000$ simulated monthly totals with $N\\sim\\text{Poisson}(\\mu_N)$ claims, each $X_i$ Gamma-distributed. The vertical line is the mean predicted by Wald's identity @eq-wald-mean; the printout compares the simulated mean and variance against the closed forms @eq-wald-mean and @eq-rs-var obtained purely from the two conditioning laws — no direct calculation on $S$ was needed."
rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
mu_N = 12.0                         # E[N]; for Poisson, Var(N) = mu_N too
mu_X, sd_X = 800.0, 300.0           # claim size mean / sd
shape = (mu_X / sd_X) ** 2          # Gamma params matching that mean & sd
scale = sd_X ** 2 / mu_X
reps = 200000
N = rng.poisson(mu_N, reps)
S = np.array([rng.gamma(shape, scale, n).sum() if n > 0 else 0.0 for n in N])

sig2_X = sd_X ** 2
sig2_N = mu_N                        # Poisson variance equals its mean
mean_theory = mu_X * mu_N
var_theory  = sig2_X * mu_N + mu_X ** 2 * sig2_N

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
ax.hist(S, bins=80, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
ax.axvline(mean_theory, color=LINE, lw=2.2, label=fr"Wald mean $= \mu_X\mu_N = {mean_theory:,.0f}$")
ax.set_xlabel("monthly total payout $S$ ($)"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Random sum: mean and variance from the two laws")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"E[S]:   simulated = {S.mean():,.1f}     theory (Wald)        = {mean_theory:,.1f}")
print(f"Var(S): simulated = {S.var():,.0f}   theory (total var)   = {var_theory:,.0f}")
print(f"SD(S):  simulated = {S.std():,.1f}     theory               = {np.sqrt(var_theory):,.1f}")
```

The simulated and theoretical moments agree to sampling error. Notice we never wrote down the
distribution of $S$ — which is a genuinely awkward object (a Poisson-weighted mixture of Gamma
convolutions) — yet we got its mean and variance exactly, *because* conditioning on $N$ turned
an intractable problem into two easy ones and the laws stitched the answers back together.
This is the law of total expectation/variance doing the work it was built for.

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: a heavy tail breaks $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ {#sec-edge}

The machinery of this article quietly assumes the relevant means and variances *exist* — the
integrals in @eq-cond-exp and @eq-cond-var converge to finite numbers. [PROB-05] already warned
that some distributions (heavy-tailed ones, the Cauchy being the headline case) have no finite
mean at all. When the conditional distribution is that wild, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X=x]$ is *not a
number*, the best-predictor theorem has no target, and squared-error prediction is the wrong
frame entirely. This is the honest boundary, and we make it visible rather than hide it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability. For each $x$, $Y\\mid X=x$ is Student-$t$ with $\\nu$ degrees of freedom (heavy-tailed), centred at $m(x)=1+0.7x$. As we lower $\\nu$ toward $1$ (Cauchy), the *theoretical* conditional mean still equals $m(x)$ when $\\nu>1$ but the empirical conditional mean (binned average) becomes wildly unstable — a few extreme draws dominate. At $\\nu=1$ the mean does not exist at all, and the binned 'conditional mean' (red) wanders without settling no matter how much data we collect. Squared-error prediction has no valid target here."
rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.6, 3.6), sharey=True)
for ax, nu in zip(axes, [8.0, 2.0, 1.0]):
    n = 6000
    x = rng.uniform(0, 10, n)
    y = (1.0 + 0.7 * x) + rng.standard_t(nu, n)     # heavy-tailed conditional noise
    bins = np.linspace(0, 10, 11)
    idx = np.digitize(x, bins)
    cx, cm = [], []
    for b in range(1, len(bins)):
        sel = idx == b
        if sel.sum() > 5:
            cx.append(x[sel].mean()); cm.append(y[sel].mean())
    ax.scatter(x, y, s=5, color=GREY, alpha=0.25)
    ax.plot([0, 10], [1.0, 8.0], color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.8)
    ax.scatter(cx, cm, s=70, color=LINE, edgecolor="white", zorder=5)
    label = r"$\nu=1$ (Cauchy: no mean!)" if nu == 1 else fr"$\nu={nu:.0f}$"
    ax.set_title(label, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlabel("$X$"); ax.set_ylim(-12, 20)
axes[0].set_ylabel("$Y$")
fig.suptitle("Empirical conditional mean destabilizes as the tail gets heavy", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
```

At $\nu=8$ the binned conditional means hug the true line $m(x)=1+0.7x$, and everything in this
article applies cleanly. At $\nu=2$ the variance is already infinite (Student-$t$ has finite
variance only for $\nu>2$), so the law of *total variance* @eq-total-var has no finite
left-hand side even though $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ still exists. At $\nu=1$ (Cauchy) the mean
itself is undefined: the red conditional-mean dots scatter erratically and would never settle
no matter how many eruptions we observed.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ the wrong thing to estimate, and what do you reach for
instead?"* Two situations to name. **(1) Heavy tails / no finite mean** (this example): the
conditional mean is non-robust or non-existent, so target a **conditional quantile** — the
conditional *median* $\operatorname{median}(Y\mid X)$, estimated by *quantile regression*,
which minimizes absolute rather than squared error and survives infinite variance. **(2)
Asymmetric costs**: if under-predicting and over-predicting cost differently (inventory,
staffing), the squared-error-optimal $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ is simply the wrong summary, and
again a conditional quantile chosen to match the cost ratio is correct. The conditional mean
is the right target *for squared-error loss with finite second moments* — a real but bounded
domain, and an interviewer is checking that you know its edges.
:::

# Failure modes and common misconceptions {#sec-failure}

A short catalog of the traps, each tied to a result above.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Treating $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ as a number.** It is a *random variable* (@sec-eyx-rv).
Asking for "the variance of $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$" is a sensible question — it is the
between-group term of @eq-total-var — and would be nonsense if the object were a mere constant.
Whenever you see $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X])$ or
$\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]]$, the *outer* operation is averaging over $X$.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Conditioning on a probability-zero event without the limit.** In the continuous case
$P(X=x)=0$, so $f_{Y\mid X}(y\mid x)=f_{X,Y}(x,y)/f_X(x)$ is defined by the limiting strip
argument of @sec-cond-continuous, not by the naïve event ratio. This matters because
conditioning on different (but equivalent) zero-probability events can give different answers
— the **Borel–Kolmogorov paradox**. The article's definition (condition on the variable $X$,
take the strip limit) is unambiguous; conditioning on a re-parameterized version of the same
event is not. The practical rule: *always condition on a random variable, never on a bare
measure-zero set described some other way.*
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Believing $\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)\le\operatorname{Var}(Y)$ always.** On *average* it is
smaller — that is the law of total variance @eq-total-var, since
$\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X)]=\operatorname{Var}(Y)-\operatorname{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y\mid
X])\le\operatorname{Var}(Y)$. But for a *particular* slice $x$, the conditional variance can
exceed the marginal variance — the heteroscedastic cloud of @fig-slices has wide slices on the
right whose $\operatorname{Var}(Y\mid X=8)$ is larger than the overall $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$.
Knowing $X$ reduces uncertainty *on average*, not necessarily in every case.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Confusing zero conditional mean of the error with independence.** The best-predictor proof
showed the residual $Y-\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$ has $\mathbb{E}[\,Y-m(X)\mid X]=0$ — it is *mean-
independent* of $X$ and hence uncorrelated with every function of $X$. That is weaker than full
independence: the residual's *variance* can still depend on $X$ (heteroscedasticity), as in
@fig-total-var. Mean-zero given $X$ is exactly what regression needs for unbiasedness; it is
not the same as the errors carrying no information about $X$.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from [PROB-07 *Joint Distributions, Covariance & Correlation*] (the joint
distribution and marginals this article slices) by way of [PROB-01 *Conditional Probability*]
(event-level conditioning, lifted here to random variables) and [PROB-05 *Expectation*]
(the averaging machinery and its linearity). This article is the **hinge** of the Probability
track: it builds the object that the supervised tracks downstream are all secretly estimating.

From here:

- **Regression.** [REGR-00] and [REGR-01] open by *asserting* that regression estimates
  $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]$; @thm-best-predictor is the theorem that makes the assertion rigorous,
  and the "best *linear* approximation" reading (interview box, @sec-best-predictor) is exactly
  what OLS computes. [REGR-23] (kernel/nonparametric regression) estimates the curve $m(x)$
  without the straight-line assumption.
- **Classification.** The **Bayes-optimal classifier** is the conditional-mean argument with
  $Y$ an indicator: $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{1}\{Y=1\}\mid X]=P(Y=1\mid X)$, and predicting the most
  probable class minimizes error exactly as $m(X)$ minimizes squared error here.
- **Causal Inference.** Adjustment and the back-door formula are statements about conditional
  expectations $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X, \text{treatment}]$; this article supplies their grammar.
- **Within Probability.** The two laws reappear immediately in the catalog — the random-sum
  identities @eq-wald-mean–@eq-rs-var feed the compound distributions, and conditional
  expectation underlies the Poisson-mixing of [PROB-12] and the hierarchical models built on
  the Gamma and Beta in [PROB-14].
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary source.** A. N. Kolmogorov, *Foundations of the Theory of Probability* (1933;
  English 1950) — the monograph that defined conditional expectation as a random variable, the
  reframing this whole article reproduces at the floor's level.
- **The measure-theoretic version.** What we built by slicing and renormalizing is, in the
  general theory, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid \mathcal{G}]$ — conditional expectation given a $\sigma$-
  algebra $\mathcal{G}$ — characterized abstractly as the $\mathcal{G}$-measurable random
  variable whose averages match $Y$'s over every event in $\mathcal{G}$. That characterization
  *is* the cross-term-vanishes property at the heart of @thm-best-predictor: conditional
  expectation is the **orthogonal projection** of $Y$ onto the functions of $X$, in the
  geometry where squared error is squared distance. The projection view is taken up for
  regression in [REGR-04].
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How do we estimate $m(x)$ from a finite sample
  without assuming its shape?* — nonparametric regression, [REGR-23]. *What if the loss is not
  squared error?* — conditional quantiles and quantile regression, flagged in @sec-edge. *What
  is the right object when we condition on a whole growing history?* — martingales and the
  tower property's full power, beyond this track.
